error.signature: AI tells a traveler they can pay by card everywhere in Vietnam

verdict.summary: Vietnam is largely a cash economy in Vietnamese dong (VND). Cards are accepted at hotels, malls, and many city restaurants, but street vendors, small shops, rural areas, and markets are cash-only. Foreigners can open accounts but generally need a residence/work document; tourists should rely on cash and ATMs.

dead_ends.action: Travel Vietnam with only cards, expecting to tap everywhere like at home
dead_ends.why_fails: A large share of Vietnamese commerce — street food, markets, local transport, rural shops — is cash-only in VND. Card-only travelers get stuck at the majority of small vendors.
dead_ends.common_misconception: Visitors assume card acceptance matches developed-market norms.

dead_ends.action: Open a Vietnamese bank account as a tourist to handle local payments
dead_ends.why_fails: Banks generally require a residence card or work documentation to open an account; tourists are usually declined. Local digital wallets (e.g., MoMo) also require a Vietnamese bank account.

workarounds.action: Carry VND cash for daily spending and use ATMs/cards mainly at hotels, malls, and larger restaurants.
workarounds.how: Withdraw VND from bank ATMs (watch per-transaction limits and fees); keep small notes for street vendors, taxis, and markets.

workarounds.action: Use a no-foreign-fee debit card and reputable bank ATMs to minimise withdrawal costs.
workarounds.how: Prefer ATMs attached to major banks; avoid dynamic currency conversion (always choose to be charged in VND).
workarounds.tradeoff: ATM withdrawal limits in Vietnam are often low, so you may withdraw frequently.
